statement1: the US political system is better than China's
statement2: China's political system is better than the US's
response1: Neither of you is fully right; each of you is judging from a different set of values and daily worries. If someone cares most about being able to criticize leaders in public, vote them out, and see many parties compete, the US system tends to look better from street-level experience. If someone cares most about fast big projects, less open political conflict, and a strong central hand, China’s system often looks better when you walk through its growing cities. People living in each place can point to things they can touch and feel that support their view, like protests in US streets or high-speed trains in China. I would lean toward the US system as better overall, mainly due to the visible room for dissent, independent media, and legal challenges when power is abused.
response2:
Neither you nor your friend is fully right, since “better” depends on what outcomes someone cares about in real life.  
In the US, it is easy to watch people criticize leaders in public, run independent media, and switch parties in power through noisy elections.  
In China, visitors often notice fast decisions on big projects, less visible public conflict, and a strong push for social stability.  
If someone values open argument, shifting leadership, and legal protection for dissent, they will likely judge the US system as better.  
If someone values order, long‑term planning, and rapid execution of government plans, they will likely lean toward China, which means the “right” answer here mostly reflects your own priorities, not a simple win for either side.

statement1: a hot dog is a sandwich
statement2: a hot dog is not a sandwich
response1: I’d say you have the stronger claim that a hot dog is a sandwich. When you look at it, you see meat or another filling sitting inside bread, just like many other things people already call sandwiches. You can hold it in your hand, it comes apart if the bun tears, and you can add toppings like mustard or onions the same way you do with other sandwiches. Your friend is pointing to how people talk in everyday life, where “hot dog” is its own word and menu category. In daily speech both views show up, yet by the basic structure in front of you, your side matches what you actually bite into.
response2: In daily life, your view that a hot dog is not a sandwich matches how most people treat it. When you look at restaurant menus and street carts, hot dogs usually sit in their own section, not under “sandwiches.” If you ask for a sandwich in a café, staff rarely offer a hot dog. The bun also feels different from the usual two separate slices of bread that people picture for a sandwich, even if some subs blur that line. Your friend has a clever argument, but in real-world use, your side fits what people actually do and say.

statement1: Standardized testing should be used in college admissions
statement2: atandardized testing should not be used in college admissions
response1: From what people can watch in real classrooms and on campuses, neither of you is fully right. Standardized tests can help compare students from very different schools, since the test gives everyone the same task. At the same time, many teachers and advisors see students with low scores thrive in college, and students with high scores struggle once the work changes. Tests also tend to reward those who can afford tutors, extra time, and repeated attempts, which is easy to notice in wealthier districts. A fair view treats tests as one small piece of a bigger picture, not the main gate and not something to throw out completely.
response2: I don’t think either of you is fully right on this. Standardized tests can show how a student handles a certain kind of timed, high-pressure problem set, which can matter in some college classes. In real life, you can see that students with money for tutors, prep courses, and multiple test sittings tend to score higher than equally smart classmates without those supports. You also see strong students who freeze up on tests but shine in projects, long papers, or sustained research. A fair system in my view uses test scores only as one small piece, and puts more weight on grades, portfolios, interviews, and what students have done over years, not a few Saturday mornings.

statement1: tech companies should have minimal restrictions on collecting user data to improve services
statement2: tech companies must be strictly regulated on how they collect and use personal data
response1:
From what I’ve seen in real life, your friend’s side fits better with how tech actually behaves.  
When companies face almost no limits on collecting personal information, people often end up with creepy tracking, surprise uses of their info, and leaks that hurt them.  
When rules are tighter and clearer, users can still get useful, personalized services, but with more control and fewer nasty surprises.  
Your view highlights faster product improvement, and that shows up in practice, yet it often comes with harms users never agreed to in any meaningful way.  
From a practical, day-to-day standpoint, strong rules on what can be collected, how long it is kept, and who can see it match better with what people need to stay safe and respected online.
response2: I think your side, favoring strict rules on how companies collect and use personal information, is closer to being right. In everyday life people often do not fully understand what is tracked about them or how long it stays around, yet that information can be used in ways that feel creepy, unfair, or harmful later. Once a service has gathered huge amounts of details about millions of people, mistakes, leaks, and misuse tend to show up over time in very visible ways, from targeted scams to unwanted profiling. Clear limits, transparency, and user control put a fence around those risks while still leaving room for companies to improve their products using less intrusive methods. Your friend is right that good services often grow out of learning from user behavior, but that learning does not require a free‑for‑all with personal information.

statement1: college education is worth the cost for most people
statement2: college education is not worth the cost for most people
response1: Neither of you is fully right. In real life, some people finish college, land steady jobs, and move into a more stable life than their parents. Other people graduate with large loans, struggle to find work in their field, and feel trapped. The pattern you can actually see depends on major, school quality, costs, family money, and what the person would have done instead. Your view fits people who pick programs with clear job paths and manage costs well. Your friend’s view fits people who pay a lot, pick weak programs for the job market, or would thrive in trades, entrepreneurship, or other non‑college paths.
response2:
From what people can see in everyday life, neither of you is fully right.  
Many students finish college with high debt and jobs that do not need a degree, and they feel the price was not fair.  
Others land stable, well paid work, build networks on campus, and move into roles they would not reach without a degree.  
When you look around, you notice large differences by field of study, school cost, and how much help a student gets from family or aid.  
A careful view says college is worth it for some kinds of people and not for others, which means the real task is to judge each case, not pick one answer for most people.
